CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized (intro, methods, experiments, conclusion) and states its main contributions clearly early on (claims about task-induced stylistic differences and a style-based classifier). Notation and formalism are not highlighted in the summary, and some reproducibility/presentation details are missing (hyperparameters not reported; code not mentioned; figures/tables not mentioned), which suggests minor gaps in presentation. Overall the writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, so only minor presentation issues prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from motivation through methods and experiments, with main contributions stated early (claims about task-induced style differences and a style-based classifier outperforming prior work). Notation and methodological description appear consistent and clear for the described logistic-regression/stylistic-feature approach, and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related work and explicit discussion of limitations (e.g., interpreting large coefficients for rare features). One minor presentation gap is that the summary does not report whether figures, tables, or examples are used or effective, suggesting possible room for improved visual/illustrative support.

Notation and methodological description appear consistent and clear for the described logistic-regression/stylistic-feature approach, and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related work and explicit discussion of limitations (e.g., interpreting large coefficients for rare features). APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses a core NLP task (ROC Story Cloze) and analyses writing style, which squarely fits top-tier NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience by combining linguistic/style analysis with empirical classification (logistic regression using character/word n-grams, POS, etc.) and compares against established baselines and prior deep-learning results. The problem formulation and evaluation (ROC Story Cloze, development set comparisons, human-judgement scores) follow community standards, and the claim of a new state-of-the-art with a simple style-based classifier is directly relevant to ongoing discussions in the venue about dataset design and task framing. | B(5): The paper studies writing style and dataset-induced stylistic artifacts in the ROC Story Cloze Task, which squarely falls within the scope of top NLP/ML venues (machine reading, style analysis, dataset bias). Its audience (NLP researchers interested in evaluation, style, and robustness) is appropriate given the use of stylistic features and a case study showing a style-based classifier outperforms prior deep models. The problem formulation (classification of story endings using stylistic features) and the evaluation against existing baselines are standard for the venue, and the work is well positioned with thorough related work coverage (notwithstanding minor reporting gaps such as missing hyperparameters/code).

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—annotation/task-induced stylistic artifacts—by empirically showing that different writing tasks produce measurable style differences and that a simple linear classifier using stylistic features (e.g., character/word n-grams, POS, sentence length) can distinguish original/right/wrong ROC story endings without context. By establishing a new state-of-the-art on the ROC Story Cloze Task with a style-based classifier, it directly challenges assumptions about what the task measures and will likely change how researchers design and evaluate narrative understanding datasets. While the contribution is focused on a single dataset, its implications for dataset design, baseline choice, and study of annotation confounds make it a strong-impact paper rather than merely niche. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem by showing that different authoring tasks produce measurable stylistic differences and that a simple style-based classifier can distinguish ROC story endings without context. Demonstrating that character and word n-grams yield a new state-of-the-art on the Story Cloze Task—outperforming prior deep learning models—directly challenges assumptions about what models learn and exposes task-design confounds in a widely used benchmark. These findings are likely to change how researchers design and evaluate datasets and models for narrative understanding and open substantive follow-up work on controlling for writing-task effects and developing more robust evaluation protocols.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—annotation/task-induced stylistic artifacts—by empirically showing that different writing tasks produce measurable style differences and that a simple linear classifier using stylistic features (e.g., character/word n-grams, POS, sentence length) can distinguish original/right/wrong ROC story endings without context. By establishing a new state-of-the-art on the ROC Story Cloze Task with a style-based classifier, it directly challenges assumptions about what the task measures and will likely change how researchers design and evaluate narrative understanding datasets. While the contribution is focused on a single dataset, its implications for dataset design, baseline choice, and study of annotation confounds make it a strong-impact paper rather than merely niche.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main empirical claims are supported by experiments on the ROC Story Cloze data showing a simple logistic-regression model using stylistic features (character and word n-grams, POS, length, punctuation) can distinguish original/right/wrong endings and achieves higher accuracy than prior deep-learning baselines. The methodology uses appropriate baselines and analyses of discriminative features, and the authors acknowledge caveats (e.g., large coefficients for rare features can be misleading). However, important experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests) which limits reproducibility and weakens confidence in the claimed performance gains. Overall the technical work appears sound but with minor methodological/reporting shortcomings. | B(4): The main claims are empirically supported: simple logistic classifiers with stylistic features distinguish original/right/wrong endings without context and identify character and word n-grams as strong signals, and the model outperforms prior deep-learning baselines on the ROC Story Cloze Task. However, important methodological details are missing (no hyperparameters or code reported) and no statistical tests are mentioned, which hinders reproducibility and assessment of significance. The authors acknowledge a key caveat about interpreting large logistic coefficients for rare features, but there remains a risk that results reflect dataset artifacts or task framing rather than general stylistic differences.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims are supported by experiments on the ROC Story Cloze data showing a simple logistic-regression model using stylistic features (character and word n-grams, POS, length, punctuation) can distinguish original/right/wrong endings and achieves higher accuracy than prior deep-learning baselines. The methodology uses appropriate baselines and analyses of discriminative features, and the authors acknowledge caveats (e.g., large coefficients for rare features can be misleading). However, important experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests) which limits reproducibility and weakens confidence in the claimed performance gains. Overall the technical work appears sound but with minor methodological/reporting shortcomings.

However, important methodological details are missing (no hyperparameters or code reported) and no statistical tests are mentioned, which hinders reproducibility and assessment of significance. The authors acknowledge a key caveat about interpreting large logistic coefficients for rare features, but there remains a risk that results reflect dataset artifacts or task framing rather than general stylistic differences.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper does not introduce a new modeling method — it applies standard stylistic features (character/word n-grams, POS, sentence length, punctuation) with a simple logistic regression — but it offers a new perspective by showing that writing-task-induced style differences allow context-free classification and even establish a new state-of-the-art on the ROC Story Cloze task compared to prior deep models. The result primarily reveals an important dataset/task-framing artifact rather than a novel algorithmic technique, so the contribution is an incremental but informative empirical finding that would likely surprise and inform experts. | B(4): The paper offers a notable and somewhat surprising perspective by showing that different writing tasks induce measurable stylistic differences and that a simple logistic-regression classifier using stylistic features (character and word n-grams, POS, length, punctuation) can distinguish original/right/wrong ROC story endings and even establish a new state-of-the-art without story context. This contribution clearly differentiates itself from prior deep-learning work on the Story Cloze Task and highlights an important dataset/task-framing confound. However, the technical approach is straightforward rather than a novel methodological advance, so the novelty lies more in the empirical finding and analysis than in new techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not introduce a new modeling method — it applies standard stylistic features (character/word n-grams, POS, sentence length, punctuation) with a simple logistic regression — but it offers a new perspective by showing that writing-task-induced style differences allow context-free classification and even establish a new state-of-the-art on the ROC Story Cloze task compared to prior deep models. The result primarily reveals an important dataset/task-framing artifact rather than a novel algorithmic technique, so the contribution is an incremental but informative empirical finding that would likely surprise and inform experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and somewhat surprising perspective by showing that different writing tasks induce measurable stylistic differences and that a simple logistic-regression classifier using stylistic features (character and word n-grams, POS, length, punctuation) can distinguish original/right/wrong ROC story endings and even establish a new state-of-the-art without story context. This contribution clearly differentiates itself from prior deep-learning work on the Story Cloze Task and highlights an important dataset/task-framing confound. However, the technical approach is straightforward rather than a novel methodological advance, so the novelty lies more in the empirical finding and analysis than in new techniques.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experiments convincingly show that simple stylistic features and linear classifiers can distinguish ending types and beat prior deep models on the ROC Story Cloze Task, and the paper includes feature-level analysis and comparisons to relevant baselines. However, there is no mention of ablation studies or sensitivity analyses (e.g., feature-set ablations or hyperparameter robustness), hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned, which hampers reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and some limitations (coefficient interpretation, task-framing confounds) are discussed, but missing experimental details and ablations constitute important gaps. | B(4): The experiments are solid: logistic regression models with a range of stylistic features are evaluated on the ROC Story Cloze Task and ROC corpus, compared to several prior baselines, and feature-level analysis (top coefficients) is used to support the main claims, yielding a new state-of-the-art. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no code mentioned, hyperparameters not reported) and there is no clear ablation or sensitivity analysis beyond reporting top-weighted features (and a caveat that coefficient interpretation can be unreliable). Related work is reported as thorough and several limitations (e.g., task framing confounds) are discussed, so the paper shows strong depth overall but has minor gaps in robustness and reproducibility analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments convincingly show that simple stylistic features and linear classifiers can distinguish ending types and beat prior deep models on the ROC Story Cloze Task, and the paper includes feature-level analysis and comparisons to relevant baselines. However, there is no mention of ablation studies or sensitivity analyses (e.g., feature-set ablations or hyperparameter robustness), hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned, which hampers reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and some limitations (coefficient interpretation, task-framing confounds) are discussed, but missing experimental details and ablations constitute important gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid: logistic regression models with a range of stylistic features are evaluated on the ROC Story Cloze Task and ROC corpus, compared to several prior baselines, and feature-level analysis (top coefficients) is used to support the main claims, yielding a new state-of-the-art. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no code mentioned, hyperparameters not reported) and there is no clear ablation or sensitivity analysis beyond reporting top-weighted features (and a caveat that coefficient interpretation can be unreliable). Related work is reported as thorough and several limitations (e.g., task framing confounds) are discussed, so the paper shows strong depth overall but has minor gaps in robustness and reproducibility analysis.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to relevant recent baselines (DSSM, LexVec, and prior deep learning models) on the same ROC Story Cloze datasets and reports evaluation using the task metric, claiming a new state-of-the-art. It also analyzes differences via stylistic feature weights and reports thorough related-work coverage. Minor shortcomings include lack of reported hyperparameters/code and no explicit acknowledgement of any prior methods that might perform comparably or better; otherwise comparisons are fair and well analyzed. | B(4): The paper compares against relevant prior work (DSSM, LexVec, and previous deep learning models on the ROC Story Cloze Task) and evaluates on the same dataset/dev set using the task’s evaluation (human judgement) as reported. However, hyperparameters and code are not reported and the authors’ method deliberately omits story context (a condition difference that may affect fairness), which are minor omissions for reproducibility and fair matching of conditions. The related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes discriminative features, but it does not clearly acknowledge cases where prior methods might perform comparably or discuss methodological differences with baselines in depth.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to relevant recent baselines (DSSM, LexVec, and prior deep learning models) on the same ROC Story Cloze datasets and reports evaluation using the task metric, claiming a new state-of-the-art. It also analyzes differences via stylistic feature weights and reports thorough related-work coverage. Minor shortcomings include lack of reported hyperparameters/code and no explicit acknowledgement of any prior methods that might perform comparably or better; otherwise comparisons are fair and well analyzed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against relevant prior work (DSSM, LexVec, and previous deep learning models on the ROC Story Cloze Task) and evaluates on the same dataset/dev set using the task’s evaluation (human judgement) as reported. However, hyperparameters and code are not reported and the authors’ method deliberately omits story context (a condition difference that may affect fairness), which are minor omissions for reproducibility and fair matching of conditions. The related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes discriminative features, but it does not clearly acknowledge cases where prior methods might perform comparably or discuss methodological differences with baselines in depth.

main_claims: Different writing tasks assigned to an author result in measurable differences in writing style.
A simple linear classifier informed with stylistic features can distinguish among original story endings, right endings, and wrong endings without using story context.
Character n-grams are among the most effective stylistic features, with word n-grams also capturing much of the signal.
A style-based classifier establishes a new state-of-the-art result on the ROC story cloze task, substantially higher than previous deep learning–based results.
_summary.methodology: Train simple linear models (logistic regression) using stylistic features (e.g., character n-grams, word n-grams, POS, sentence length, punctuation) to classify types of story endings in the ROC story cloze setup; analyze most discriminative features via highest absolute logistic regression coefficients.
baselines: previous deep learning models (Salle et al., 2016 and others)
limitations: Interpreting highest logistic regression coefficients may be unreliable because linear models can assign large coefficients to rare features.
Different writing task framings can confound dataset design and classification problems (methodological challenge).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - writing style / story cloze task (machine reading)
